(sometimes abbreviated as **VFX**) is the process by which imagery is created or manipulated outside the context of a live-action shot in [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") and [video production](/wiki/Video_production "Video production").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Various processes by which imagery is created

"VFX" redirects here. For the 1968 US Navy fighter program, see [VFX (aircraft)](/wiki/VFX_(aircraft) "VFX (aircraft)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Visual_effects "Special:EditPage/Visual effects") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Visual effects"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Visual+effects%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Visual+effects%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Visual+effects%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Visual+effects%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Visual+effects%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Visual+effects%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Visual effects** (sometimes abbreviated as **VFX**) is the process by which imagery is created or manipulated outside the context of a live-action shot in [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") and [video production](/wiki/Video_production "Video production").
The integration of live-action footage and other live-action footage or [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") (CGI) elements to create realistic imagery is called VFX.

VFX involves the integration of live-action footage (which may include in-camera special effects) and generated-imagery (digital or optics, animals or creatures) which look realistic, but would be dangerous, expensive, impractical, time-consuming or impossible to capture on film. Visual effects using CGI have more recently become accessible to the independent filmmaker with the introduction of affordable and relatively easy-to-use [animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation") and [compositing](/wiki/Compositing "Compositing") software.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Special effect](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect")

### Early developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/L%27hommealat%C3%AAtet.jpg/250px-L%27hommealat%C3%AAtet.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27hommealat%C3%AAtet.jpg)

*The Man with the Rubber Head*

In 1857, [Oscar Rejlander](/wiki/Oscar_Gustave_Rejlander "Oscar Gustave Rejlander") created the world's first "special effects" image by combining different sections of 32 negatives into a single image, making a [montaged](/wiki/Photomontage "Photomontage") [combination print](/wiki/Combination_print "Combination print"). In 1895, [Alfred Clark](/wiki/Alfred_Clark_(director) "Alfred Clark (director)") created what is commonly accepted as the first-ever motion picture special effect. While filming a reenactment of the beheading of [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"), Clark instructed an actor to step up to the block in Mary's costume. As the executioner brought the axe above his head, Clark stopped the camera, had all the actors freeze, and had the person playing Mary step off the set. He placed a Mary dummy in the actor's place, restarted filming, and allowed the executioner to bring the axe down, severing the dummy's head. Techniques like these would dominate the production of special effects for a century.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

It was not only the first use of trickery in cinema, it was also the first type of photographic trickery that was only possible in a motion picture, and referred to as the "[stop trick](/wiki/Stop_trick "Stop trick")". [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès"), an early motion picture pioneer, accidentally discovered the same "stop trick".

According to Méliès, his camera jammed while filming a street scene in Paris. When he screened the film, he found that the "stop trick" had caused a truck to turn into a hearse, pedestrians to change direction, and men to turn into women. Méliès, the director of the [Théâtre Robert-Houdin](/wiki/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_Robert-Houdin "Théâtre Robert-Houdin"), was inspired to develop a series of more than 500 short films, between 1896 and 1913, in the process developing or inventing such techniques as [multiple exposures](/wiki/Multiple_exposure "Multiple exposure"), [time-lapse photography](/wiki/Time-lapse_photography "Time-lapse photography"), [dissolves](/wiki/Dissolve_(filmmaking) "Dissolve (filmmaking)"), and hand-painted color.

Because of his ability to seemingly manipulate and transform reality with the [cinematograph](/wiki/Cinematograph "Cinematograph"), the prolific Méliès is sometimes referred to as the "Cinemagician". His most famous film, *[Le Voyage dans la lune](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")* (1902), a whimsical parody of [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne")'s *[From the Earth to the Moon](/wiki/From_the_Earth_to_the_Moon "From the Earth to the Moon")*, featured a combination of live action and [animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation"), and also incorporated extensive [miniature](/wiki/Scale_model "Scale model") and [matte painting](/wiki/Matte_painting "Matte painting") work.

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern")]

VFX today is heavily used in almost all movies produced. Other than films, television series and web series are also known to utilize VFX.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Techniques")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Madame_Nobel_-_film_set_at_the_Embassy_of_France_in_Vienna_May_2014_08.jpg/250px-Madame_Nobel_-_film_set_at_the_Embassy_of_France_in_Vienna_May_2014_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madame_Nobel_-_film_set_at_the_Embassy_of_France_in_Vienna_May_2014_08.jpg)

A [period drama](/wiki/Period_drama "Period drama") set in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") uses a [green screen](/wiki/Chroma_key "Chroma key") as a backdrop, to allow a background to be added during [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production").

* [Special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect"): Special effects (often abbreviated as SFX, SPFX, F/X or simply FX) are illusions or visual tricks used in the [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), [film](/wiki/Film "Film"), [television](/wiki/Television "Television"), [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") and [simulator](/wiki/Simulator "Simulator") industries to simulate the fictional events in a [story](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") or [virtual world](/wiki/Virtual_world "Virtual world"). With the emergence of digital film-making, a distinction between special effects and visual effects has grown, with the latter referring to digital [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production") while "special effects" refers to mechanical and optical effects. Mechanical effects (also called [practical](/wiki/Practical_effect "Practical effect") or [physical effects](/wiki/Physical_effects "Physical effects")) are usually accomplished during the live-action shooting. This includes the use of mechanized [props](/wiki/Theatrical_property "Theatrical property"), scenery, [scale models](/wiki/Scale_model "Scale model"), [animatronics](/wiki/Animatronics "Animatronics"), [pyrotechnics](/wiki/Pyrotechnics "Pyrotechnics") and atmospheric effects: creating physical wind, rain, fog, snow, clouds, making a car appear to drive by itself and blowing up a building, etc. Mechanical effects are also often incorporated into set design and makeup. For example, [prosthetic makeup](/wiki/Prosthetic_makeup "Prosthetic makeup") can be used to make an actor look like a non-human creature. Optical effects (also called photographic effects) are techniques in which images or film frames are created photographically, either "in-camera" using [multiple exposures](/w/index.php?title=Multiple_exposures&action=edit&redlink=1 "Multiple exposures (page does not exist)"), [mattes](/wiki/Matte_(filmmaking) "Matte (filmmaking)"), or the [Schüfftan process](/wiki/Sch%C3%BCfftan_process "Schüfftan process") or in post-production using an [optical printer](/wiki/Optical_printer "Optical printer"). An optical effect might place actors or sets against a different background.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Activemarker2.PNG/250px-Activemarker2.PNG)](/wiki/File:Activemarker2.PNG)

[Motion Capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture"): A high-resolution uniquely identified active marker system with 3,600 × 3,600 resolution at 960 hertz providing real-time submillimeter positions

* [Motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture"): Motion-capture (sometimes referred as mo-cap or mocap, for short) is the process of recording the [movement](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)") of objects or people. It is used in [military](/wiki/Military_science "Military science"), [entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment "Entertainment"), [sports](/wiki/Sports "Sports"), medical applications, and for validation of computer vision[[3]](#cite_note-3) and robotics.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") and [video game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development"), it refers to recording actions of [human actors](/wiki/Motion_capture_acting "Motion capture acting"), and using that information to animate [digital character](/wiki/Digital_character "Digital character") models in 2-D or 3-D [computer animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-twsBackstage-6)[[7]](#cite_note-twsGuardian-7) When it includes face and fingers or captures subtle expressions, it is often referred to as performance capture.[[8]](#cite_note-twsWired-8) In many fields, motion capture is sometimes called motion tracking, but in filmmaking and games, motion tracking usually refers more to [match moving](/wiki/Match_moving "Match moving").
* [Matte painting](/wiki/Matte_painting "Matte painting"): A matte painting is a painted representation of a landscape, set, or distant location that allows filmmakers to create the illusion of an environment that is not present at the filming location. Historically, matte painters and film technicians have used various techniques to combine a matte-painted image with live-action footage. At its best, depending on the skill levels of the artists and technicians, the effect is "seamless" and creates environments that would otherwise be impossible or expensive to film. In the scenes the painting part is static and movements are integrated on it.
* [Animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation"): Animation is a method in which [figures](/wiki/Image "Image") are manipulated to appear as moving images. In [traditional animation](/wiki/Traditional_animation "Traditional animation"), images are drawn or painted by hand on transparent [celluloid sheets](/wiki/Cel "Cel") to be photographed and exhibited on [film](/wiki/Film "Film"). Today, most animations are made with [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") (CGI). [Computer animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation") can be very detailed [3D animation](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics"), while [2D computer animation](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") can be used for stylistic reasons, low bandwidth or faster [real-time renderings](/wiki/Real-time_rendering "Real-time rendering"). Other common animation methods apply a [stop-motion](/wiki/Stop-motion "Stop-motion") technique to two and three-dimensional objects like [paper cutouts](/wiki/Cutout_animation "Cutout animation"), [puppets](/wiki/Puppet "Puppet") or [clay figures](/wiki/Clay_animation "Clay animation"). Swift progression of consecutive images with minor differences is a common approach to achieving the stylistic look of animation. The illusion—as in motion pictures in general—is thought to rely on the [phi phenomenon](/wiki/Phi_phenomenon "Phi phenomenon") and [beta movement](/wiki/Beta_movement "Beta movement"), but the exact causes are still uncertain. [Analog](/wiki/Analog_device "Analog device") mechanical animation media that rely on the rapid display of sequential images include the [phénakisticope](/wiki/Phenakistiscope "Phenakistiscope"), [zoetrope](/wiki/Zoetrope "Zoetrope"), [flip book](/wiki/Flip_book "Flip book"), [praxinoscope](/wiki/Praxinoscope "Praxinoscope") and film. [Television](/wiki/Television "Television") and [video](/wiki/Video "Video") are popular electronic animation media that originally were analog and now operate [digitally](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media"). For display on the computer, techniques like [animated GIF](/wiki/Animated_GIF "Animated GIF") and [Flash animation](/wiki/Flash_animation "Flash animation") were developed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Then_%26_Now_-_Main_Hall.jpg/250px-Then_%26_Now_-_Main_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Then_%26_Now_-_Main_Hall.jpg)

Composite of photos of one place, made more than a century apart

* [3D modeling](/wiki/3D_modeling "3D modeling"): In [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics"), 3-D modeling is the process of developing a mathematical representation of any *[surface](/wiki/Surface_(mathematics) "Surface (mathematics)")* of an object (either inanimate or living) in [three dimensions](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space") via [specialized software](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics_software "3D computer graphics software"). The product is called a 3-D model. Someone who works with 3-D models may be referred to as a 3-D artist. It can be displayed as a two-dimensional image through a process called *[3D rendering](/wiki/3D_rendering "3D rendering")* or used in a [computer simulation](/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") of physical phenomena. The model can also be physically created using [3D printing](/wiki/3D_printing "3D printing") devices.
* [Rigging](/wiki/Skeletal_rigging "Skeletal rigging"): Skeletal animation or rigging is a technique in [computer animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation") in which a [character](/wiki/Character_(animation) "Character (animation)") (or another articulated object) is represented in two parts: a surface representation used to draw the character (called the *[mesh](/wiki/Polygon_mesh "Polygon mesh")* or *skin*) and a hierarchical set of interconnected parts (called *bones*, and collectively forming the *skeleton* or *rig*), a virtual [armature](/wiki/Armature_(sculpture) "Armature (sculpture)") used to animate (*pose* and *key-frame*) the mesh.[[9]](#cite_note-9) While this technique is often used to animate humans and other organic figures, it only serves to make the animation process more intuitive, and the same technique can be used to control the deformation of any object—such as a door, a spoon, a building, or a galaxy. When the animated object is more general than, for example, a humanoid character, the set of "bones" may not be hierarchical or interconnected but simply represent a higher-level description of the motion of the part of the mesh it is influencing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Green_screens_compare_with_Iman_Crosson_20110524.png/250px-Green_screens_compare_with_Iman_Crosson_20110524.png)](/wiki/File:Green_screens_compare_with_Iman_Crosson_20110524.png)

Green-screen compositing is demonstrated by actor [Iman Crosson](/wiki/Iman_Crosson "Iman Crosson") in a self-produced video.  
**Top panel:** A frame in a full-motion video shot in the actor's living room.[[10]](#cite_note-10)  
**Bottom panel:** The corresponding frame in the final version in which the actor impersonates [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") "appearing" outside the White House's East Room.[[11]](#cite_note-SPOOFvid-11)

* [Rotoscoping](/wiki/Rotoscoping "Rotoscoping"): Rotoscoping is an [animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation") technique that [animators](/wiki/Animators "Animators") use to trace over motion picture footage, [frame](/wiki/Frame_(film) "Frame (film)") by frame, to produce realistic action. Originally, animators projected photographed live-action movie images onto a [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") panel and traced over the image. This projection equipment is referred to as a *rotoscope*, developed by Polish-American animator [Max Fleischer](/wiki/Max_Fleischer "Max Fleischer"). This device was eventually replaced by computers, but the process is still called rotoscoping. In the visual effects industry, *rotoscoping* is the technique of manually creating a [matte](/wiki/Matte_(filmmaking) "Matte (filmmaking)") for an element on a live-action plate so it may be [composited](/wiki/Digital_compositing "Digital compositing") over another background.[[12]](#cite_note-PM-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Chroma key](/wiki/Chroma_key "Chroma key") is more often used for this, as it is faster and requires less work, however, rotoscope is still used on subjects that are not in front of a green (or blue) screen, due to practical or economic reasons.
* [Match Moving](/wiki/Match_moving "Match moving"): In visual effects, match-moving is a technique that allows the insertion of computer graphics into [live-action](/wiki/Live-action "Live-action") footage with correct position, scale, orientation, and motion relative to the photographed objects in the shot. The term is used loosely to describe several different methods of extracting camera [motion](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)") information from a [motion picture](/wiki/Motion_picture "Motion picture"). Sometimes referred to as motion-tracking or camera-solving, match moving is related to [rotoscoping](/wiki/Rotoscoping "Rotoscoping") and [photogrammetry](/wiki/Photogrammetry "Photogrammetry"). Match moving is sometimes confused with [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture"), which records the motion of objects, often human actors, rather than the camera. Typically, motion capture requires special cameras and sensors and a controlled environment (although recent developments such as the [Kinect](/wiki/Kinect "Kinect") camera and [Apple's](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") [Face ID](/wiki/Face_ID "Face ID") have begun to change this). Match moving is also distinct from [motion control photography](/wiki/Motion_control_photography "Motion control photography"), which uses mechanical hardware to execute multiple identical camera moves. Match moving, by contrast, is typically a software-based technology applied after the fact to normal footage recorded in uncontrolled environments with an ordinary camera. Match moving is primarily used to track the movement of a camera through a shot so that an identical virtual camera move can be reproduced in a [3D animation](/wiki/3D_animation "3D animation") program. When new CGI elements are composited back into the original live-action shot, they will appear in a perfectly matched perspective.
* [Compositing](/wiki/Compositing "Compositing"): Compositing is the combining of visual elements from separate sources into single images, often to create the illusion that all those elements are parts of the same scene. [Live-action](/wiki/Live-action "Live-action") shoots for compositing is variously called "[chroma key](/wiki/Chroma_key "Chroma key")", "blue screen", "green screen" and other names. Today, most, though not all, compositing is achieved through [digital image](/wiki/Digital_image "Digital image") manipulation. Pre-[digital compositing](/wiki/Digital_compositing "Digital compositing") techniques, however, go back as far as the trick films of [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès") in the late 19th century, and some are still in use.
* [Splash of color](/wiki/Splash_of_color "Splash of color"): The term splash of color is the use of a colored item on an otherwise monochrome film image.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Production pipeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Production pipeline")]

Visual effects are often integral to a movie's story and appeal. Although most visual effects work is completed during [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production"), it usually must be carefully planned and choreographed in [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production") and [production](/wiki/Film_production "Film production"). While [special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect") such as explosions and car chases are made on [set](/wiki/Theatrical_scenery "Theatrical scenery"), visual effects are primarily executed in post-production with the use of multiple tools and technologies such as graphic design, modeling, animation and similar software. A [visual effects supervisor](/wiki/Visual_effects_supervisor "Visual effects supervisor") is usually involved with the production from an early stage to work closely with production and the film's [director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director") to design, guide and lead the teams required to achieve the desired effects.

### Visual effects companies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_effects&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Visual effects companies")]

See also: [List of visual effects companies](/wiki/List_of_visual_effects_companies "List of visual effects companies")

Many studios specialize in visual effects; among them are [Digital Domain](/wiki/Digital_Domain "Digital Domain"), [DreamWorks](/wiki/DreamWorks_Pictures "DreamWorks Pictures"), [DNEG](/wiki/DNEG "DNEG"), [Framestore](/wiki/Framestore "Framestore"), [Weta Digital](/wiki/Weta_Digital "Weta Digital"), [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic"), [Pixomondo](/wiki/Pixomondo "Pixomondo"), [Moving Picture Company](/wiki/Moving_Picture_Company "Moving Picture Company"), [Animal Logic](/wiki/Animal_Logic "Animal Logic"), [Reel FX Animation](/wiki/Reel_FX_Animation "Reel FX Animation"), [Sony Pictures Imageworks](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Imageworks "Sony Pictures Imageworks") and [Jellyfish Pictures](/wiki/Jellyfish_Pictures "Jellyfish Pictures").
